name: backends
context: Configure remote backends to store Terraform state outside the local filesystem with encryption and access controls
info: Terraform backends determine where state is stored and how operations are executed. The default local backend writes terraform.tfstate to disk, but production teams must use remote backends for shared access, encryption, and locking. Popular backends include S3 (with DynamoDB for locking), GCS, Azure Blob Storage, Consul, PostgreSQL, and Terraform Cloud (remote). Backends are configured in the `terraform {}` block and initialized with `terraform init`. Changing backends requires `terraform init -migrate-state` to transfer existing state. Partial configuration with `-backend-config` flags or files keeps secrets out of version control. See [[terraform/state]] for state fundamentals and [[terraform/state/locking]] for how backends implement locking.
triggers: set up remote state for terraform
body:
# Terraform State Backends

A backend defines where Terraform stores its state file and, optionally, where it executes operations. The default `local` backend writes `terraform.tfstate` to your working directory. For any team or CI/CD workflow, you must configure a remote backend.

## Backend Types

| Backend | Storage | Locking | Auth |
|---------|---------|---------|------|
| `local` | Local filesystem | No | N/A |
| `s3` | AWS S3 bucket | DynamoDB table | IAM / env vars |
| `gcs` | Google Cloud Storage | Native | Service account / ADC |
| `azurerm` | Azure Blob Storage | Native blob lease | Service principal / CLI |
| `consul` | Consul KV store | Native | ACL token |
| `pg` | PostgreSQL database | Advisory locks | Connection string |
| `http` | Any HTTP endpoint | Optional | Custom headers |
| `remote` | Terraform Cloud/Enterprise | Native | API token |

## S3 Backend with DynamoDB Locking

The most common production setup for AWS teams. S3 stores the state file; DynamoDB provides distributed locking:

```hcl
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
```

The S3 bucket should have:
- **Versioning enabled** — allows state rollback if corruption occurs
- **Server-side encryption** — AES-256 or KMS for encryption at rest
- **Bucket policy** — restrict access to authorized IAM roles only
- **Block public access** — prevent accidental public exposure

The DynamoDB table requires a partition key named `LockID` of type String. See [[terraform/state/locking]] for details.

## GCS Backend

For Google Cloud teams:

```hcl
terraform {
  backend "gcs" {
    bucket  = "my-terraform-state"
    prefix  = "prod"
  }
}
```

GCS provides native state locking without an additional table. Authenticate via `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` environment variable or Application Default Credentials.

## Azure Blob Backend

For Azure teams:

```hcl
terraform {
  backend "azurerm" {
    resource_group_name  = "terraform-state-rg"
    storage_account_name = "tfstatestorage"
    container_name       = "tfstate"
    key                  = "prod.terraform.tfstate"
  }
}
```

Azure Blob Storage uses native blob leases for locking. Authenticate via `ARM_ACCESS_KEY`, `ARM_CLIENT_ID` + `ARM_CLIENT_SECRET` + `ARM_TENANT_ID`, or Azure CLI.

## Terraform Cloud Backend

```hcl
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "my-org"
    workspaces {
      name = "prod"
    }
  }
}
```

Terraform Cloud provides state storage, locking, run history, policy enforcement, and team access controls in a managed service. The `cloud` block (Terraform 1.1+) replaces the older `backend "remote"` syntax.

## Partial Backend Configuration

Keep sensitive values out of version control using `-backend-config` flags or files:

```bash
# Pass individual values
terraform init \
  -backend-config="bucket=my-terraform-state" \
  -backend-config="key=prod/terraform.tfstate" \
  -backend-config="region=us-east-1"

# Use a config file
terraform init -backend-config=backend.hcl
```

```hcl
# backend.hcl (do NOT commit this file)
bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
region         = "us-east-1"
dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
encrypt        = true
```

Add `backend.hcl` to `.gitignore`. In CI/CD, inject values from environment variables or a secrets manager.

## Backend Migration

When changing backends (e.g., local to S3), Terraform migrates state automatically:

```bash
# Update the backend block in your config, then:
terraform init -migrate-state

# Terraform will prompt:
#   "Do you want to copy existing state to the new backend?"
# Answer "yes" to transfer state.
```

To reconfigure without migration (start fresh):

```bash
terraform init -reconfigure
```

## Environment Variables for Backend Auth

Avoid hardcoding credentials in backend blocks:

| Variable | Backend | Purpose |
|----------|---------|----------|
| `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` | s3 | IAM access key |
| `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | s3 | IAM secret key |
| `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN` | s3 | Temporary session token |
| `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` | gcs | Path to service account JSON |
| `ARM_ACCESS_KEY` | azurerm | Storage account access key |
| `ARM_CLIENT_ID` | azurerm | Service principal client ID |
| `ARM_CLIENT_SECRET` | azurerm | Service principal secret |
| `ARM_TENANT_ID` | azurerm | Azure AD tenant ID |
| `TF_TOKEN_app_terraform_io` | remote/cloud | Terraform Cloud API token |

sections.title: S3 backend with DynamoDB locking
sections.body: The S3 backend stores state in an AWS S3 bucket with optional DynamoDB-based locking (see [[terraform/state/locking]]). Configure `bucket`, `key` (the state file path within the bucket), `region`, `dynamodb_table`, and `encrypt = true`. Enable S3 versioning for state rollback. Use a bucket policy restricting access to authorized IAM roles. The DynamoDB table needs a `LockID` string partition key.

sections.title: GCS backend
sections.body: The GCS backend stores state in a Google Cloud Storage bucket with native locking. Configure `bucket` and `prefix` (directory path). Authenticate via `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` or Application Default Credentials. GCS provides object versioning for state history and built-in encryption.

sections.title: Azure Blob Storage backend
sections.body: The `azurerm` backend stores state in Azure Blob Storage with native blob lease locking. Configure `resource_group_name`, `storage_account_name`, `container_name`, and `key`. Authenticate via `ARM_ACCESS_KEY` for storage key auth, or `ARM_CLIENT_ID` + `ARM_CLIENT_SECRET` + `ARM_TENANT_ID` for service principal auth. Azure CLI auth is also supported.

sections.title: Partial backend configuration
sections.body: Use `-backend-config` flags or files to supply sensitive backend values at init time instead of hardcoding them in HCL. Pass individual key=value pairs with `-backend-config="key=value"` or reference a `.hcl` file with `-backend-config=backend.hcl`. Add the config file to `.gitignore`. In CI/CD, inject backend values from environment variables or a secrets manager to keep credentials out of version control.

sections.title: Backend migration
sections.body: Change backends by updating the `terraform {}` block and running `terraform init -migrate-state`. Terraform prompts to copy existing state to the new backend. Use `-reconfigure` to reinitialize without migrating state (starts fresh). Always back up your state before migrating — run `terraform state pull > backup.tfstate` first. Migration moves both state data and any workspace configurations.

sections.title: Terraform Cloud and Enterprise
sections.body: The `cloud` block (Terraform 1.1+) connects to Terraform Cloud for managed state storage, locking, run history, policy-as-code (Sentinel/OPA), and team collaboration. Specify `organization` and `workspaces`. Authenticate via `terraform login` or the `TF_TOKEN_app_terraform_io` environment variable. Terraform Cloud also supports remote execution, where plan and apply run on Terraform Cloud infrastructure instead of locally.

links.context: State fundamentals — what state tracks and why it exists

related.note: Understanding state is required before configuring backends
Locking is a backend capability that prevents concurrent modifications

scripts.name: Setup S3 Backend with DynamoDB Locking
scripts.description: Creates an S3 bucket with versioning and encryption, a DynamoDB table for state locking, and outputs the backend configuration block
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Setup S3 Backend with DynamoDB Locking for Terraform State
# Required env: AWS_REGION, STATE_BUCKET, DYNAMODB_TABLE
# Optional env: AWS_PROFILE

REGION="${AWS_REGION:?AWS_REGION is required}"
BUCKET="${STATE_BUCKET:?STATE_BUCKET is required}"
TABLE="${DYNAMODB_TABLE:-terraform-locks}"

echo "==> Creating S3 bucket: ${BUCKET} in ${REGION}"
if [ "$REGION" = "us-east-1" ]; then
  aws s3api create-bucket \
    --bucket "$BUCKET" \
    --region "$REGION"
else
  aws s3api create-bucket \
    --bucket "$BUCKET" \
    --region "$REGION" \
    --create-bucket-configuration LocationConstraint="$REGION"
fi

echo "==> Enabling versioning on ${BUCKET}"
aws s3api put-bucket-versioning \
  --bucket "$BUCKET" \
  --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled

echo "==> Enabling default encryption on ${BUCKET}"
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption \
  --bucket "$BUCKET" \
  --server-side-encryption-configuration '{
    "Rules": [{
      "ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault": {
        "SSEAlgorithm": "AES256"
      },
      "BucketKeyEnabled": true
    }]
  }'

echo "==> Blocking public access on ${BUCKET}"
aws s3api put-public-access-block \
  --bucket "$BUCKET" \
  --public-access-block-configuration \
    BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true

echo "==> Creating DynamoDB table: ${TABLE} in ${REGION}"
aws dynamodb create-table \
  --table-name "$TABLE" \
  --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \
  --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \
  --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST \
  --region "$REGION" 2>/dev/null || echo "    Table already exists (OK)"

echo "==> Waiting for DynamoDB table to become active..."
aws dynamodb wait table-exists --table-name "$TABLE" --region "$REGION"

echo ""
echo "==> Backend ready. Add this to your Terraform configuration:"
echo ""
cat <<EOF
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "${BUCKET}"
    key            = "<env>/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "${REGION}"
    dynamodb_table = "${TABLE}"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
EOF

echo ""
echo "==> Then run: terraform init -migrate-state"
echo "==> Done."

AWS_REGION.description: AWS region for the S3 bucket and DynamoDB table

STATE_BUCKET.description: Name of the S3 bucket to create for state storage

DYNAMODB_TABLE.description: Name of the DynamoDB table for state locking

AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID.description: IAM access key with S3 and DynamoDB permissions

bucket_or_container.description: S3 bucket name, GCS bucket name, or Azure container name

key.description: State file path within the bucket or container

encrypt.description: Enable server-side encryption for state at rest

backend_configured.description: Whether the backend was successfully configured

bucket_created.description: Whether the storage bucket/container was created

backend_block.description: HCL backend configuration block to add to Terraform config

depends.reason: Understanding state is required before configuring backends
IAM policies needed for S3 backend access control
